story:
To most passersby, he was just an old man shuffling along at his own stubborn pace. To him, each careful step on the smooth concrete was a calculation, arthritis tugging at his joints, osteoporosis nipping at his bones, cataracts dimming his view of the red and orange trees and the yellowing bushes under a mist-thick fall sky. He debated the cane again, maybe a walker—he could probably get one donated. He sighed, knowing each concession was another nod to time.

Saturday, or Sunday—perhaps Friday? The day never mattered as much as the destination. Every week he made this walk to his beloved’s home. He’d chosen this senior facility for that one reason: he could reach the cemetery on his own two feet. He laughed at the illusion of choice. The other places demanded shuttles that never ran on time, caregivers he called babysitters, and fees that made no sense. What was he looking for—tennis courts?

He paused to drink in the small things. The greens and yellows glowed like neon through the mist. Birds chattered nearby and far away—he counted it a blessing that his hearing still held. Yet something was different. He could not name it.

He kept to the concrete’s edge where weeds pressed through the cracks. On most mornings, a jogger or a young couple would pass. Some said hello, some gave him room, others scrolled past as if he weren’t there. Today, no one. No birds in sight either, not even a bug. The air was brisk, not cold—the kind people loved for exercise. Maybe it was Monday and he’d started late. Maybe.

Ten more feet and the cemetery gates welcomed him. Ten more minutes at his speed and he would stand at her side again. He carried no flowers this time; mobility and money were thin, and the nurses’ scolding after his falls still stung. Losing his keys and his license had hurt worst of all. His body held his freedom hostage; his mind sometimes joined in.

At last he reached her stone, beside the empty plot reserved for him. He closed his eyes and offered a quiet prayer: for her peace, for her not to worry about him anymore. Then he shuffled to the ornate bench and sat. That was when he knew for certain: something had changed.

It was more serene than usual, the mist now a light fog. Warmth spread through him. The familiar ache did not climb his joints. Peace settled in as a well-dressed man took the open space beside him.

“Morning,” the stranger said, voice rough, then steady. He nodded toward the marker. “Your wife?”

He nodded. “Is your love here too?”

“They are,” the man said, eyes forward, brown and bright beneath gray brows. His suit was impeccable, the sort sold on streets with names.

“How long were you together?” the man asked, as if she still lived. He softened the look. “You haven’t spoken of her in a long while, have you? She passed in 2012.”

“I hold on to my memories,” he murmured. “When I speak of her, most just nod.”

“Polite nods? Or hearts listening?”

“I still have faith,” he said, smiling. “Though my story may feel like one they’ve heard before—or one I’ve told and forgotten.”

“So tell me the day you met. Would you change anything?”

He frowned at the odd request, then obliged. He’d been coming off a factory shift, stopping on a whim for a drink. A Friday, a day like this. She was the sunshine, a waitress on a pickup shift. If he could do it again, he’d have been cleaned up, dressed like he was now—as if he had an appointment that could change his life.

“Are you ready?” the man asked.

“For death?” he joked softly. “My body’s been ready for years.”

“Have you noticed?” the man pressed. “The weather. The people who once made space but now pass as if you’re not there.”

His voice lowered. “It’s been weeks, I think.”

“Where are you?”

“I’m here,” he said, steadying himself. “At my wife’s resting place. Don’t treat me like a fool. My memory fails me, but this I know.”

“In,” the man said. “Are you sure?” He stood, hands in pockets. “No matter the place or time, you find happiness where you want to be.” Then he walked into the fog and was gone.

The old man tilted his head to the sky. “I’m sorry, my love, you had to hear that.”

“I’m glad I was there,” a familiar voice whispered.

He turned. She stood in sunlight, flowered dress glowing, blue eyes lifting his heart as always. He smiled, properly dressed at last. Then he saw the new stone beside hers, his name carved with a date from two months prior.

He was right where he was supposed to be—only a little late for his appointment.
